checks.a_analysis: The output text follows the source document closely. The title in metadata is a standard AI-generated identifier based on document content. The headings and paragraphs are all derived directly from the source text. There are no invented sections or body text.
There is no contact information (phone, email, URL) in either the source or the output. Therefore, no fabrication occurred.
All main sections of the resolution (WHEREAS clauses, RESOLVED clause, date, and signature blocks) are present in the output. No pages were dropped from the conversion.

The output uses a table structure to represent the signature blocks at the bottom of the document. The source does not have a formal table, but the conversion into key-value pairs in a table format correctly captures the two signees (Chairman and Clerk) and their respective titles. All data rows required for this structural interpretation are present.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: The paragraphs and headings are complete. While a single word label is missing (noted in C6), it does not constitute a significant omission of a section's content (well below the 25% threshold).

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text 'ATTEST:' which appears in the source document preceding the Clerk to the Board signature block is missing from the output JSON. While the signature block for Susan O. Washburn itself is present in the table (element index 10), the specific label indicating the attestation is omitted.
checks.b_evidence_location: Signature block area, immediately before the Clerk's signature, before element index 10

checks.a_analysis: I have reviewed the output JSON and compared it to the source document. Each text block (header, title, clauses, date, and signature information) appears exactly once in the JSON, mirroring its single appearance in the source image. There are no instances of duplicated paragraphs or repeated sections.

The output uses a logical heading structure. The main document title ('RESOLUTION SUPPORTING...') is correctly tagged as a level 1 heading (Element index 2). The preceding text identifying the location is treated as a paragraph, which is appropriate. There are no skipped heading levels or incorrect nesting.

The source document does not contain any formal tables. It contains signature blocks at the bottom. The pipeline has organized these signature blocks into a table structure (Element index 10) for clarity and accessibility. The structure of this generated table is internally consistent (2 columns, 3 rows including an AI-generated header) and correctly associates the titles/roles with the names. Since no pre-existing table was corrupted, and the conversion of signature blocks to a table is a valid structural interpretation for accessibility, this is not a defect.

The reading order in the JSON (Elements 1-11) follows the logical top-to-bottom, left-to-right flow of the source document. It starts with the geographic headers, moves to the title, then the 'WHEREAS' clauses, the resolution statement, the date, the signature area, and finally the seal image. This accurately reflects the source's intended information flow.
The source document does not contain any formal lists (bulleted or numbered). The 'WHEREAS' clauses are individual paragraphs, and they are rendered as such in the output.
There are no links in the source document, and no links were fabricated or injected into the output JSON.

checks.a_analysis: The visual organization of the converted HTML faithfully follows the source PDF. The document title, preamble, 'WHEREAS' clauses, and resolution statement are presented in the same logical sequence. The conversion of signatures into a 'Board of Commissioners Signatures' table is an acceptable way to represent this information in an accessible HTML format. The single-column flow is appropriate for a responsive HTML layout.

The 'Board of Commissioners Signatures' table is well-rendered. It clearly distinguishes between the printed field name/title and the transcribed name of the signatory. The borders and layout are clean, making the data easy for a sighted user to read and understand.

The meaningful image in the source document is the Rockingham County seal. While this seal is not converted into an individual image element within the structured HTML portion of the output, it is visible in the full-page image of the source document appended below the text. Therefore, the image content is technically present in the output, though its placement is redundant due to content duplication (see V4).

The entire content of the resolution is visually duplicated on the page. The document is first rendered as accessible HTML text and a signature table. Immediately following this, the entire source PDF is included as a full-page image. This results in every part of the document—the title, all 'WHEREAS' clauses, the resolution text, and the signature blocks—appearing twice in the body of the output.
checks.b_evidence_source: The document contains a single instance of the resolution text from 'RESOLUTION SUPPORTING THE RECLASSIFICATION...' to 'CLERK TO THE BOARD'.
checks.b_evidence_output: The entire content of the resolution is rendered twice: once as HTML text and a table, and a second time as a full-page image immediately following the signature table.
checks.b_evidence_location: Throughout the document; the duplication begins after the 'Board of Commissioners Signatures' table.
